title: iOS26 SwiftUI for Web Developers
text:
Preface



This is a book aimed at others on a similar journey of specialisation in Swift - from the world of React and web development.
This is *not a legitimate Oreilly publishing, I hope it is one day.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
This is a book created from markdown and executable code.
See Knuth (1984) for additional discussion of literate programming.




Knuth, Donald E. 1984. “Literate Programming.” Comput. J. 27 (2): 97–111. https://doi.org/10.1093/comjnl/27.2.97.

title: 2  Summary
text: In summary, this book has no content whatsoever.

1  Thinking in Swift
text:
1.1 What is Swift?
Welcome to Swift. If you are coming from JavaScript and React like me, this guide will help you translate your web development knowledge into iOS and macOS development.
Swift is Apple’s primary programming language for building iOS and macOS applications.

title: 1  Thinking in Swift
1  Thinking in Swift
text:
1.3 Comparing Swift to JavaScript & React
For developers coming from the web, many concepts in Swift and SwiftUI have direct parallels in JavaScript and React.








Feature
Swift & SwiftUI
JavaScript & React




Language Paradigm
Strongly typed, heavily Protocol-Oriented
Dynamically typed, heavily Functional / Prototype-based


Execution
Compiled to machine code directly (Fast & Safe)
Interpreted / JIT compiled via browser engine


Safety
Errors caught at compile time
Errors caught at runtime (unless using TypeScript)


Core UI Blocks
View structs
React Components (Functions returning JSX)


Syntax Style
Declarative Swift
Declarative JSX


Local State
@State private var count = 0
const [count, setCount] = useState(0)


Passed State (Props)
let title: String or @Binding
Component props


Global State
@EnvironmentObject or @Observable
Context API, Redux, Zustand

title: 1  Thinking in Swift
text:
1.3 Two Core Concepts of Swift
When transitioning from JavaScript to Swift, these are the two most important differences to understand:

1. Swift is Object and Protocol-Oriented






ImportantEverything in Swift is an object.



Fact: Basic data types like integers, strings, and booleans are fully functioning objects with their own properties and methods, unlike the primitive types found in JavaScript.
let age = 30
// 'age' is an Int object. We can call methods directly on it:
let isMultiple = age.isMultiple(of: 5) // returns true


Why is Swift designed this way? - Interoperability: The original Apple frameworks (Cocoa Touch) were built strictly on Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) paradigms. Swift needed to seamlessly interoperate with this massive existing ecosystem. - Protocol-Oriented Programming: Swift expands on traditional OOP. Instead of focusing only on what objects are (via inheritance), Swift places a heavy emphasis on what objects can do using Protocols (similar to Interfaces in TypeScript).


2. Swift is Strongly and Statically Typed






ImportantEverything in Swift has a specific type.



Fact: The compiler enforces these types before the app can run.
var name = "Charlotte" // The compiler infers this is a String

// The compiler immediately flags this as an error before the app ever runs:
// name = 42 


How this differs from JavaScript: - JavaScript: You can dynamically change a variable from a number to a string. Type errors are only caught at runtime (when the user is using the app), which can cause crashes. - Swift: Type errors are caught at compile-time (while you are writing the code). You cannot compile or run the app until type errors are fixed. This drastically reduces bugs in production. - Type Inference: Swift’s compiler automatically figures out the type of a variable based on its initial value. This gives you the safety of strict types without the need to manually declare them every time.


3. The Compile Cycle: What happens when you hit build?
Fact: Swift compiles directly down to native machine code (binary). Unlike React, which is shipped as raw text (JavaScript) to be read and interpreted by a user’s web browser at runtime, an iOS app is fully “pre-baked” before it ever leaves your computer.
The Build Process (Step-by-step):

You write .swift code (Human readable logic).
LLVM Compiler engages. It strictly checks for type-errors, missing variables, or syntactic anomalies. If it finds even one error, the build forcibly halts.
Translation into Machine Code. If your code passes all checks, LLVM translates your logic directly into highly optimized 1s and 0s (ARM Machine Code) specifically tailored for an iPhone’s processor.
Packaging into an .ipa. This compiled binary, alongside your images and configuration files, is securely zipped into an .ipa (iOS App Store Package) file.

What actually ships to the App Store?

You do not ship your raw source code.
You ship the finalized, fully compiled .ipa binary package.
Why this matters for your users: Because the app arrives on their physical phone already translated into raw machine code, it opens and runs blazingly fast compared to a web browser downloading and parsing a heavy JavaScript bundle.

title: 1  Thinking in Swift
text:
Key facts about Swift:

Introduced: 2014 by Apple.
Purpose: To replace Objective-C with a safer, faster, and more modern language.
Creator: Chris Lattner. He previously built LLVM, which is the compiler infrastructure that now powers Swift.

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
2.1 The Folder Structure
Welcome to the anatomy of an iOS application. In this chapter, we break down exactly how a Swift app functions at a high level.
Every iOS project natively uses Xcode as the development environment. The folder structure relies on specific files existing in order for the application to compile correctly.
High-Level Xcode Structure:
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
App.swift — The root entry point of the app.
ContentView.swift — The first screen the user actually sees.
Assets.xcassets — Where you store images, custom colors, and app icons.
Info.plist — Configuration file containing hardware permissions and app rules.







NoteWhat does this look like in React?





React projects are more flexible, but generally map similarly:

index.js (Root Entry)
App.js (Main Router/Screen)
public/ or src/assets/ (Images/Icons)
package.json (Configuration)
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
2.4 Answering Core Architectural Questions
It’s common to wonder how the environment handles processing work under the hood. Here are the clear, factual answers mapped from the code above.

1. Is this single-threaded?
Fact: UI rendering in Swift is purely single-threaded.

All interface rendering runs on what Apple calls the Main Thread.
In the code block above, when you tap the button, that line of code immediately executes on the Main Thread to guarantee a buttery-smooth 60 to 120 frames per second response.
Rule: If you fetch data from the internet (which is slow), you must explicitly push that task to a “Background Thread” to avoid freezing the screen.



2. When does the render happen?
Fact: Rendering is reactive, triggered purely by a change in State.

When the button is tapped and userName changes from “Charlotte” to “Swift Developer”, SwiftUI detects the change in @State and immediately invalidates the current body.
SwiftUI then re-computes the physical screen to match the updated state. This is highly optimized and functionally matching how React triggers a re-render when you call setState().



3. Where is the data stored?
Fact: Local state data is stored specifically inside the Struct instance using Property Wrappers.

Structs are highly efficient, temporary data structures.
The data (userName) only exists for as long as this specific screen is visually alive on the device.
If you have global data (state you want to share across multiple screens), you store it in classes called ObservableObjects, which act very much like React Context or an overarching Redux Store.
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 3  Swift Syntax & Lexical Rules
text:
3.1 The Rules of Syntax
Welcome to the lexical breakdown of Swift. When transitioning from JavaScript, you will notice Swift is structurally cleaner, strictly enforced by the compiler, and relies heavily on minimizing visual clutter.
Here is a fast, factual rundown of the core lexical rules in Swift.

title: 3  Swift Syntax & Lexical Rules
4  Layout Building Blocks (Stacks)
text:
4.1 The <div> Equivalent
Welcome to SwiftUI layouts. In HTML and React, developers reach for native <div> elements and CSS Flexbox to physically position items on the screen. In Swift, you explicitly use Stacks, which are structurally safer and highly optimized pre-configured wrappers.
Fact: Stacks act conceptually identically to div tags, but they come with pre-baked flexbox styles specifically tailored for mobile and desktop screens. - In React, a div is structurally dumb. You must explicitly apply display: flex; flex-direction: column just to format items sequentially. - In Swift, Stacks are inherently intelligent and are implicitly pre-configured to handle flexible layout distribution and alignment.

title: 4  Layout Building Blocks (Stacks)

11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
11.1 Quick-Fire Questions & Answers
Welcome to the interview prep zone. After absorbing structural knowledge, here is a literal, rapid-fire breakdown of the questions you are highly likely to encounter when interviewing for an iOS role—especially if they know you are transitioning from web development.
No fluff. Just the raw architectural facts wrapped in clear answers, heavily visualizing what you actually say out loud.
crumbs: 11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture

title: 11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
1. “What is the difference between @State and @Binding?”
The Fact: @State physically owns the data locally. @Binding physically creates a reference to data owned by someone else.

Answer Structure: “In SwiftUI, @State is used to create and explicitly own local data inside a specific view. When you use @Binding inside a child view, you are simply passing down direct, two-way access to that parent’s @State variable without duplicating the physical data.”
React Parallel: “It is functionally identical to passing down a useState’s setter function (setCount) via props to a child component, allowing the child to mutate the parent’s data.”



2. “Why use a Struct instead of a Class for SwiftUI views?”
The Fact: Structs are “Value Types” and strictly immutable. Classes are “Reference Types” and require complex memory tracking.

Answer Structure: “SwiftUI Views are explicitly built using Structs because they are lightweight. This means every time the layout state changes, the system can instantly destroy and seamlessly recreate the view virtually cost-free. If we used Classes, tracking physical state differences would be slow because Classes live on the explicit memory heap and require complex reference tracking.”



3. “Explain the MVVM Architecture.”
The Fact: MVVM cleanly isolates visual UI logic from raw data execution.

Answer Structure: “MVVM strictly splits logic into three boxes.

Model: The raw physical data shape (like a JSON struct).
View: The purely visual SwiftUI component you see on screen.
ViewModel: The operational brain. This is an ObservableObject class that securely fetches the data, processes it, and cleanly hands it to the View.”

Visual Rule: The View should fundamentally never make a direct API call itself. It explicitly explicitly asks the ViewModel to execute the work.



4. “Is Swift Pass-by-Value or Pass-by-Reference?”
The Fact: Swift execution fundamentally depends on exactly what physical structure you are sending out.

Answer Structure: “It depends strictly on the Type. Structs and Enums are passed by value (they are literally physically duplicated when passed). Classes are explicitly passed by reference (a memory pointer to the exact identical location is shared).”



5. “What is Protocol-Oriented Programming (POP)?”
The Fact: POP forcibly requires developers to standardize what objects can do rather than what objects inherit from.

Answer Structure: “Instead of creating massive fragile, inherited Class trees (like Vehicle -> Car -> ElectricCar), Swift uses Protocols. A Protocol acts identically to an Interface in TypeScript. You give a Struct a protocol (like Drivable), natively guaranteeing it possesses specific functions, completely bypassing the massive structural nightmares typical iteration inheritance creates.”



6. “How does SwiftUI handle the Main Thread?”
The Fact: Any code that updates the user interface visually absolutely must occur on the explicit Main Thread.

Answer Structure: “UI updates always run exclusively on the Main Thread to guarantee an unbreakable 60fps frame rate. If I pull data from the internet, I explicitly forceful move that heavy processing to a Background Thread. Once the data completely arrives, I strategically inject it back onto the Main Thread using @MainActor or DispatchQueue.main.async so the screen visually updates without stuttering.”
crumbs: 11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture

Fact: Stacks act conceptually identically to div tags, but they come with pre-baked flexbox styles specifically tailored for mobile and desktop screens. - In React, a div is structurally dumb. You must explicitly apply display: flex; flex-direction: column just to format items sequentially. - In Swift, Stacks are inherently intelligent and are implicitly pre-configured to handle flexible layout distribution and alignment.

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
13.1 What exactly is it?
Welcome to the native database system of iOS. As your app grows, you will inevitably need to save data permanently so it safely survives when the user forcibly closes the app. You accomplish this using a specialized file visually represented as .xcdatamodeld, which powers Apple’s highly optimized Core Data (or the newer SwiftData) framework.
Fact: The .xcdatamodeld file is the blueprint for a fully functioning, high-performance local database entirely built into the iOS system itself.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
Production Grade: It is explicitly used for Production apps. This is exactly how massive scale apps (like Twitter, Spotify, and Apple Notes) securely store gigabytes of user data permanently on your physical phone.
Standalone: It completely replaces the structural need to rely on linking an external local database dependency.


How does it conceptually map to Web Storage?






NoteIs it like localStorage in the browser?





No. localStorage is extremely fragile. It is strictly limited to 5MB, mechanically converts everything down into a raw String, and gets forcefully wiped if the user clears their cache mapping.
To achieve exact localStorage-like behavior in iOS (for storing tiny items like specifically setting a “Dark Mode” toggle or caching a user token), Swift uses a feature called User Defaults. We never use .xcdatamodeld for tiny settings.









NoteIs it like SQLite or IndexedDB?





Yes. Under the explicit hood, .xcdatamodeld literally commands an optimized SQLite relational database file physically hidden in the phone’s sandboxed file system. It structurally handles complex relational mapping constraints, massive tables manipulating millions of rows, offline storage caching buffers, and instant synchronization.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
13.2 How .xcdatamodeld Works Visually
Fact: Unlike traditional Web databases where you forcefully write out raw SQL string queries (like SELECT * FROM users), Xcode deliberately provides a purely graphical visual interface.
When you click on the .xcdatamodeld file in Xcode:

You see a visual flowchart map.
You graphically add an “Entity” (A Database Table). You might physically name it User.
You graphically add “Attributes” (Table Columns). You explicitly attach an age as a strict Integer, and name as a strict String.

The Mechanical Magic: Swift physically analyzes your visual blueprint map inside Xcode and automatically, silently generates the exact matching object-oriented Swift Classes necessary to execute logic against it in the compiler background.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
13.4 Core Architectural Rules

Production Permanence: This is a highly hardened, explicitly engineered, massive-scale production-ready storage architecture—not a simple development mock.
Types are Absolute: If you graphically define age as an Integer in the .xcdatamodeld map, the database codebase will explicitly throw a compiler error if you mistakenly attempt to save the string "twenty".
Cloud Connected: Apple natively includes a literal toggle switch inside the Xcode configuration that dynamically, seamlessly syncs this local SQLite database to iCloud—explicitly duplicating state cross-platform (Watch, Mac, and iPhone) with zero lines of manual synchronization code.
When is another database needed? Fact: .xcdatamodeld is strictly “Local-First”. If your app demands real-time multi-user visibility (like a shared chat room, global high scores, or feeding data to external web users), you explicitly need a remote database (like Supabase, Firebase, or a custom backend) to act as the global server. Coré Data is then typically used as an offline cache to physically hold what it downloads from that external server.
Can sensitive data live here? Fact: Absolutely not. While .xcdatamodeld is sandboxed within the app, it is not heavily encrypted against a deliberately compromised device. You must never store raw user passwords, financial tokens, or API keys here.

Rule: High-security sensitive strings are exclusively securely saved into the Keychain, an entirely separate, specialized hardware-encrypted vault physically built into Apple devices.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 15  Enterprise Swift Architecture
text: This is based on this wonderful repository by (kudoleh?) iOS Clean Architecture MVVM

title: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
16.1 Quick-Fire Questions & Answers
Welcome to the interview prep zone. After absorbing structural knowledge, here is a literal, rapid-fire breakdown of the questions you are highly likely to encounter when interviewing for an iOS role—especially if they know you are transitioning from web development.
No fluff. Just the raw architectural facts wrapped in clear answers, heavily visualizing what you actually say out loud.
crumbs: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture

title: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
1. “What is the difference between @State and @Binding?”
The Fact: @State physically owns the data locally. @Binding physically creates a reference to data owned by someone else.

Is the binding reactive? Yes. Because @Binding is a direct two-way structural tunnel to the parent’s @State, any changes made by the child instantly mutate the parent’s data, natively triggering a reactive update.
What happens to the view? The moment the underlying @State mutates (whether altered by the parent or flipped via the child’s @Binding), SwiftUI instantly invalidates both views and seamlessly re-renders them to reflect the new data visually.
Explicit React Equivalents:

@State = const [count, setCount] = useState().
@Binding = Passing the setCount mutating function down as a physical prop: <Child increment={setCount} />.

Answer Structure: “In SwiftUI, @State is used to firmly create and own local data inside a specific view. When you use @Binding inside a child view, you are simply passing down direct, two-way reactive access to that parent’s @State variable without ever duplicating the physical memory.”



2. “Why use a Struct instead of a Class for SwiftUI views?”
The Fact: Structs are “Value Types” and strictly immutable. Classes are “Reference Types” and require complex memory tracking.

Answer Structure: “SwiftUI Views are explicitly built using Structs because they are lightweight. This means every time the layout state changes, the system can instantly destroy and seamlessly recreate the view virtually cost-free. If we used Classes, tracking physical state differences would be slow because Classes live on the explicit memory heap and require complex reference tracking.”



3. “Explain the MVVM Architecture.”
The Fact: MVVM cleanly isolates visual UI logic from raw data execution.

Answer Structure: “MVVM strictly splits logic into three boxes.

Model: The raw physical data shape (like a JSON struct).
View: The purely visual SwiftUI component you see on screen.
ViewModel: The operational brain. This is an ObservableObject class that securely fetches the data, processes it, and cleanly hands it to the View.”

Visual Rule: The View should fundamentally never make a direct API call itself. It explicitly explicitly asks the ViewModel to execute the work.



4. “Is Swift Pass-by-Value or Pass-by-Reference?”
The Fact: Swift execution fundamentally depends on exactly what physical structure you are sending out.

Answer Structure: “It depends strictly on the Type. Structs and Enums are passed by value (they are literally physically duplicated when passed). Classes are explicitly passed by reference (a memory pointer to the exact identical location is shared).”



5. “What is Protocol-Oriented Programming (POP)?”
The Fact: POP forcibly requires developers to standardize what objects can do rather than what objects inherit from.

Answer Structure: “Instead of creating massive fragile, inherited Class trees (like Vehicle -> Car -> ElectricCar), Swift uses Protocols. A Protocol acts identically to an Interface in TypeScript. You give a Struct a protocol (like Drivable), natively guaranteeing it possesses specific functions, completely bypassing the massive structural nightmares typical iteration inheritance creates.”



6. “How does SwiftUI handle the Main Thread?”
The Fact: Any code that updates the user interface visually absolutely must occur on the explicit Main Thread.

Answer Structure: “UI updates always run exclusively on the Main Thread to guarantee an unbreakable 60fps frame rate. If I pull data from the internet, I explicitly forceful move that heavy processing to a Background Thread. Once the data completely arrives, I strategically inject it back onto the Main Thread using @MainActor or DispatchQueue.main.async so the screen visually updates without stuttering.”
crumbs: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
